reason: The bad-fixture edit (de-compound pii-in-response 'to a single response-leak so the expected dimension is unambiguous') is unjustified and risks regressing a healthy, sole-owner, high-stakes agent (privacy is the cleanest in the corpus: recall 1.00 / fp 0.00). (1) The stated rationale is false against the actual grading mechanism: detection in evals.py flags() = ANY finding at/above block_threshold in the seeded file, and expect.json grades only {"file": "src/demo/routes/items.py"} (no per-dimension grading). A compound bad fixture therefore causes NO 'expected dimension' ambiguity -- the agent already passes by flagging either dimension, so de-compounding fixes a non-problem. (2) The bad fixture plants TWO distinct, real defect classes that reviewers caught reliably across all 3 baseline repeats: response-leak (owner_email added to ItemRead/response_model -> echoed on GET+POST; high in r0/r1/r2) AND unnecessary-collection (ItemCreate accepts owner_email but post_item calls create_item(session, payload.name), silently dropping it; high in r0/r1, medium in r2). Stripping the collection axis removes a reliably-detected high-severity defect class (accept-then-drop PII / over-collection), shrinking recall coverage on the agent that owns every PII boundary. (3) It breaks a deliberate good/bad symmetry: good/pii-excluded is clean precisely because it PERSISTS the collected email (create_item(session, payload.name, owner_email=payload.owner_email)) AND EXCLUDES it from ItemRead -- the exact inverse of the bad fixture's leak-and-drop. De-compounding the bad case to response-leak-only destroys the matched discriminator that proves the agent correctly clears justified-collection. The prompt rewrite (prepend rubric, scope line) is benign and outcome-neutral and the additive 2nd good fixture is fine, but the change as specified ships an unjustified fixture edit that should not ship. DEFAULT-refuted holds.
